name: Soapstone Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Soapstone. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Soapstone cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Soapstone mineral absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without substrate damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse width for controlled ablation of Soapstone surface contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning

passCount.description: Recommended number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for industrial cleaning of soft stones like soapstone to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100 W for controlled ablation. Max limited by soapstone's low thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/mK) to prevent cracking. Source: General Nd:YAG laser specs from manufacturers like IPG Photonics and academic reviews on stone conservation (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds for effective contaminant removal on delicate soapstone surfaces; range adjusts for light (faster) vs. heavy contamination. Recommended 1000 mm/s balances efficiency and safety. Source: Industry standards from laser cleaning equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems) and process optimization studies.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of minerals to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond/femtosecond for ultra-precision but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended for soapstone's low melting point (~1300°C) to reduce thermal diffusion. Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015) on ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Mid-range frequencies for efficient cleaning without excessive heat buildup on low-conductivity soapstone; higher rates speed up process but risk accumulation. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019).

energy_density.description: Fluence below soapstone's ablation threshold (~4-5 J/cm² estimated for talc-based minerals) for safe contaminant removal; conservative range due to variable reflectivity at 1064 nm. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for effective cleaning without substrate damage. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Appl. Phys. (2017) on silicate rocks; uncertainty noted for exact soapstone composition.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots for precise cleaning on soapstone's textured surface; range for fine detail (smaller) vs. large areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage without overheating. Source: Equipment specifications from laser cleaning systems (e.g., CleanLaser CL series).

pass_count.description: Multi-pass for thorough removal of contaminants on porous soapstone; single pass for light dirt, up to 5 for heavy. Recommended 2 passes to ensure complete cleaning while minimizing exposure. Source: Industry case studies on stone restoration (e.g., Restaurators 2020).

overlap_ratio.description: Higher overlap ensures uniform coverage on irregular soapstone surfaces; 50-80% typical for even energy distribution. 70% recommended to avoid gaps without excessive passes. Source: Process optimization studies in laser scanning (e.g., Precis. Eng. 2016).
